learning_objectives: Understand the HTTP request-response cycle and how browsers render web pages
Write semantic HTML5 documents with proper document structure and accessibility attributes
Use browser developer tools to inspect the DOM, network requests, and rendering performance
Distinguish between presentational and semantic markup and explain why semantic HTML matters
readings: MDN Web Docs: HTTP Overview
MDN Web Docs: Introduction to HTML
MDN Web Docs: HTML elements reference (semantic elements section)

lecture_notes.introduction: The web is the most widely deployed application platform in history, running on every device from phones to televisions to automobiles. This course teaches you to build for it properly, from the ground up. We begin with HTML, the structural foundation of every web page. HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is not a programming language; it is a markup language that describes the structure and meaning of content. The distinction between structure and presentation is fundamental: HTML defines what content is (a heading, a paragraph, a navigation menu, an article), while CSS defines how it looks (colors, fonts, layout). When you use a <nav> element instead of a <div>, you communicate to browsers, screen readers, and search engines that this content is a navigation menu. This semantic information enables accessibility features (screen readers announce 'navigation' to visually impaired users), search engine optimization (Google uses semantic structure to understand page content), and future-proofing (new devices and browsers can render your content appropriately because they understand its meaning). The HTTP protocol underpins all web communication: your browser sends a request (GET /index.html), the server sends a response (200 OK with the HTML), and the browser parses the HTML into a DOM tree, fetches linked resources (CSS, JS, images), and renders the page. Understanding this cycle is essential for debugging, performance optimization, and building web applications.
key_concepts: HTTP request-response cycle: client sends request (method, URL, headers, body), server returns response (status code, headers, body); every web page load involves multiple HTTP requests
HTTP methods: GET (retrieve), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove) — each with specific semantics and idempotency guarantees
HTTP status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 301 Moved Permanently, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error
HTML5 semantic elements: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, <footer>, <figure>, <figcaption>, <time>, <mark> — each communicates meaning beyond visual presentation
Document structure: <!DOCTYPE html>, <html lang='en'>, <head> (metadata, title, links), <body> (visible content); proper nesting and valid structure
Accessibility foundations: alt text for images, proper heading hierarchy (h1-h6 in order), form labels associated with inputs, ARIA attributes for complex interactive components
The DOM: the browser's tree representation of HTML that JavaScript can manipulate; the bridge between static markup and dynamic behavior
Browser developer tools: Elements tab (inspect and modify DOM), Network tab (monitor HTTP requests), Console tab (JavaScript debugging), Performance tab (rendering analysis)
Web standards: W3C specifications, browser compatibility, progressive enhancement (core content works everywhere, enhanced features for modern browsers)
HTML validation: using the W3C validator to catch structural errors that browsers silently fix but that may cause unpredictable rendering
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a semantic HTML page for a restaurant website: header with logo and navigation, main content with articles for menu sections (appetizers, entrees, desserts), each menu item in a figure with figcaption, contact section with a form, and footer. Inspect the page with Chrome DevTools: examine the DOM tree, use the Accessibility tab to verify screen reader experience, switch to the Network tab and trace the full HTTP request-response cycle showing request headers, response status, and content type.
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension installed
Chrome or Firefox with Developer Tools
Node.js 20+ installed for later sessions
GitHub account configured for assignment submission

review.value: Semantic elements (nav, article, aside, figure) communicate meaning to browsers, screen readers, and search engines. Using <div> for everything is technically valid but sacrifices accessibility and SEO benefits. Choose the element that describes the content's role.

Each HTTP request is independent with no memory of previous requests. Cookies, sessions, and tokens are mechanisms layered on top of HTTP to maintain state across requests. This statelessness simplifies the protocol but complicates application development.

Build the core experience with HTML (works everywhere), enhance with CSS (looks better), then add JavaScript (interactive). If JavaScript fails to load, the HTML content is still accessible. This approach maximizes resilience and accessibility.

The DOM is the live, in-memory representation of HTML that the browser maintains. JavaScript manipulates the DOM to create dynamic pages. Understanding the DOM is essential for debugging rendering issues and understanding framework internals.

learning_objectives: Use Flexbox and CSS Grid for page layout without float-based hacks
Implement responsive designs using media queries and mobile-first methodology
Apply CSS custom properties, transitions, and modern styling techniques
Debug layout issues using browser DevTools CSS inspection
readings: MDN Web Docs: CSS Flexbox
MDN Web Docs: CSS Grid Layout
MDN Web Docs: Responsive design (media queries and viewport units)

lecture_notes.introduction: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls the visual presentation of HTML content. Modern CSS has evolved dramatically from the float-based layout hacks of the early web. Flexbox and CSS Grid are now universally supported layout systems that make complex layouts achievable with clean, readable code. Flexbox handles one-dimensional layouts (a row of cards, a navigation bar), while Grid handles two-dimensional layouts (a page with sidebar, content area, and footer). Responsive design ensures that layouts adapt gracefully to any screen size, from a 4-inch phone to a 34-inch ultrawide monitor. The mobile-first approach starts by designing for the smallest screen (which forces you to prioritize content) and then uses min-width media queries to add complexity for larger screens. This produces simpler CSS (the base styles are the mobile styles, with progressive enhancements for larger screens) and better performance on mobile devices (which do not download or parse styles they do not use if structured correctly). CSS custom properties (variables) enable consistent theming by defining colors, spacing, and typography values once and referencing them throughout the stylesheet. Transitions and animations add polish: a button that smoothly changes color on hover feels more responsive than one that snaps between states. This session gives you the tools to build any layout you can imagine without resorting to JavaScript for positioning.
key_concepts: Flexbox: one-dimensional layout with justify-content (main axis), align-items (cross axis), flex-wrap, flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis for precise control
CSS Grid: two-dimensional layout with grid-template-columns, grid-template-rows, grid-template-areas (named areas), gap, grid-column/grid-row (spanning), and fr unit (fractional)
Media queries: @media (min-width: 768px) for tablet, @media (min-width: 1024px) for desktop; prefer min-width for mobile-first approach
Mobile-first design: write base CSS for mobile (no media queries needed), then progressively add styles for larger screens; this forces content prioritization and produces leaner CSS
CSS custom properties: --primary-color: #2563eb; usage: color: var(--primary-color); cascade through the DOM, can be overridden per-component or via media queries for dark mode
The box model: content, padding, border, margin; box-sizing: border-box makes sizing intuitive by including padding and border in the element's width
CSS specificity: inline > ID > class > element; understanding specificity prevents selector wars and makes debugging easier
Transitions and transforms: transition: all 0.3s ease; transform: scale(1.05) for smooth hover effects; hardware-accelerated with transform and opacity
CSS architecture: BEM (Block Element Modifier) naming convention (.card, .card__title, .card--featured) for maintainable, predictable class names at scale
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a responsive dashboard layout from scratch: use CSS Grid for the overall page layout (sidebar, header, main content, footer), Flexbox for the navigation bar and card rows within the content area. Implement three breakpoints: single-column mobile layout, two-column tablet layout with collapsible sidebar, and three-column desktop layout with fixed sidebar. Use CSS custom properties for theming, add smooth transitions on card hover, and test at all three widths using DevTools responsive mode.
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension
Browser DevTools responsive design mode (Ctrl+Shift+M in Chrome)
CSS reset or normalize.css

review.value: Use Flexbox for one-dimensional layouts (a row of cards, a navigation bar). Use Grid for two-dimensional layouts (a page layout with header, sidebar, content, and footer). They work together: Grid for the page structure, Flexbox for component internals.

Write CSS for the smallest screen first (no media queries), then add min-width media queries for larger screens. This produces simpler CSS, better mobile performance, and forces you to prioritize content for the most constrained viewport.

CSS custom properties are runtime values that cascade through the DOM and can be changed with JavaScript. Preprocessor variables (Sass $variable) are compile-time only. Custom properties enable dynamic theming (dark mode toggle) that preprocessor variables cannot.

Block__Element--Modifier naming convention (.card__title--highlighted) creates a flat, predictable class structure that avoids specificity wars. Each class is self-documenting: you know the component, the part, and the variant from the class name alone.

learning_objectives: Write modern JavaScript using ES6+ features including let/const, arrow functions, destructuring, and template literals
Understand JavaScript's type system, scope rules, closures, and the event loop
Use Promises and async/await for asynchronous programming
Apply array methods (map, filter, reduce) for functional-style data transformation
readings: MDN Web Docs: JavaScript Guide (Grammar and Types through Expressions and Operators)
Eloquent JavaScript, 3rd Ed., Ch. 1-5, 11
MDN Web Docs: Using Promises

lecture_notes.introduction: JavaScript is the programming language of the web. It runs in every browser, and with Node.js, it runs on servers too. Originally designed in 10 days in 1995, JavaScript has evolved into a powerful, expressive language that supports multiple programming paradigms: imperative, object-oriented, and functional. Modern JavaScript (ES6+, standardized in 2015 and updated annually) includes features that make the language significantly more pleasant to write and read: block-scoped variables (let, const), arrow functions for concise callbacks, destructuring for extracting values from objects and arrays, template literals for string interpolation, and modules for organizing code into reusable files. JavaScript is dynamically typed (variables can hold any type) and single-threaded with an event loop that handles asynchronous operations. Understanding the event loop is crucial because web applications are inherently asynchronous: network requests, user interactions, timers, and animations all happen concurrently. Promises and async/await provide a structured way to write asynchronous code that reads like synchronous code, avoiding the nested callback chains ('callback hell') that plagued earlier JavaScript. Array methods like map, filter, and reduce enable functional-style data transformation that is more declarative and less error-prone than manual for loops.
key_concepts: let vs const vs var: const for values that do not change (default choice), let for values that do, var is function-scoped (legacy, avoid); block scoping prevents accidental variable leakage
Arrow functions: const add = (a, b) => a + b; concise syntax, lexical 'this' binding (no more 'var self = this' workaround)
Destructuring: const { name, age } = person; const [first, ...rest] = array; extract values from objects and arrays concisely
Template literals: `Hello ${name}, you have ${count} items`; multi-line strings and expression interpolation without concatenation

The event loop: call stack executes synchronous code, Web APIs handle async operations (setTimeout, fetch, DOM events), callback queue holds completed callbacks, event loop moves callbacks to the call stack when it is empty
Promises: represent the eventual result of an asynchronous operation; states: pending, fulfilled, rejected; chainable with .then() and .catch()
async/await: syntactic sugar over Promises; async function returns a Promise, await pauses execution until Promise resolves; try/catch for error handling
Array methods: map (transform each element), filter (select elements matching a condition), reduce (accumulate elements into a single value), find (first matching element), some/every (boolean tests)
Closures: a function that captures variables from its enclosing scope; the foundation of data privacy, module patterns, and callback factories in JavaScript
Spread and rest operators: ...array spreads elements, ...params collects remaining arguments; used for array copying, object merging, and flexible function signatures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a data dashboard that fetches user data from a public API (JSONPlaceholder), processes it using array methods (filter active users, map to display format, reduce to calculate statistics), and handles errors with try/catch. Show the event loop in action: log before fetch, log after fetch, see that the 'after' log appears before the data arrives because fetch is asynchronous. Demonstrate how async/await makes the same code read sequentially.
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension
Browser console for interactive experimentation
JSONPlaceholder API (https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com) for practice data

review.value: JavaScript is single-threaded but non-blocking. Async operations (fetch, setTimeout) are delegated to Web APIs, which place callbacks in the queue when complete. The event loop moves callbacks to the call stack only when it is empty, which is why synchronous code always runs before async callbacks.

Syntactic sugar over Promises that makes asynchronous code read like synchronous code. 'await' pauses execution of the async function until the Promise resolves. try/catch handles rejections. Always use async/await over raw .then() chains for readability.

const prevents reassignment of the variable binding, not mutation of the value. const arr = [1,2,3]; arr.push(4) is legal because the array reference did not change. Use Object.freeze() or libraries for true immutability.

Array methods (map, filter, reduce) are declarative: they say what to do, not how. A for loop says how step by step. Declarative code is shorter, less error-prone (no off-by-one bugs), and easier to read once you learn the methods.

learning_objectives: Select, create, modify, and remove DOM elements programmatically
Handle user events using addEventListener with proper event delegation
Understand event bubbling, capturing, and propagation control
Build interactive components without a framework to understand what frameworks abstract
readings: Eloquent JavaScript, Ch. 13-15 (The DOM, Handling Events, Project: A Platform Game)
MDN Web Docs: Introduction to events
MDN Web Docs: Event reference

lecture_notes.introduction: The DOM (Document Object Model) is the live, in-memory tree representation of an HTML document that the browser maintains. When you write JavaScript that changes the DOM, the browser re-renders the affected parts of the page. This is the foundation of all dynamic web content: adding items to a shopping cart, showing validation errors on a form, loading new content without a page refresh, and animating elements in response to user actions. Before React and other frameworks existed, every interactive web page was built by directly manipulating the DOM with vanilla JavaScript. Understanding DOM manipulation is essential even if you primarily use frameworks, because frameworks are abstractions over the DOM, and abstractions leak. When React behaves unexpectedly, understanding what it does under the hood (creating DOM elements, attaching event listeners, diffing virtual DOM trees) lets you debug effectively. Event handling is equally fundamental: every user interaction (click, keypress, scroll, form submission, mouse movement) fires an event that propagates through the DOM tree. Event delegation, the practice of attaching a single event listener to a parent element and using event.target to identify which child triggered the event, is a powerful pattern that handles dynamically added elements and reduces memory usage.
key_concepts: DOM selection: document.querySelector('selector') returns the first match, document.querySelectorAll('selector') returns a NodeList of all matches; CSS selectors for precise targeting
DOM creation: document.createElement('tag'), element.textContent = 'text', element.setAttribute('key', 'value'), parent.appendChild(child), parent.insertBefore(child, reference)
DOM modification: element.classList.add/remove/toggle('class'), element.style.property = 'value', element.innerHTML (dangerous: XSS risk) vs element.textContent (safe)
DOM removal: element.remove() or parent.removeChild(child); clearing children with while (parent.firstChild) parent.removeChild(parent.firstChild)

Event handling: element.addEventListener('click', handler); the handler receives an Event object with target (element that triggered), currentTarget (element listener is on), and methods like preventDefault() and stopPropagation()
Event bubbling: events fire on the target element, then propagate upward through ancestors (child -> parent -> grandparent -> document); this enables event delegation
Event delegation: attach one listener to the parent, check event.target to identify which child was clicked; handles dynamically added elements automatically and uses less memory
Form handling: submit event on the form element, FormData API for extracting values, preventDefault() to prevent page reload, client-side validation before submission
XSS prevention: never use innerHTML with user-supplied data; use textContent for text, createElement for structure; sanitize any HTML that must be rendered from external sources

Performance: batch DOM modifications using DocumentFragment or by modifying elements off-DOM, then inserting once; avoid reading layout properties (offsetHeight) between writes (forces layout thrashing)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a dynamic task manager with vanilla JavaScript: (1) a form to add tasks with title, priority, and due date, (2) tasks render in a list with complete and delete buttons, (3) event delegation on the task list handles clicks on dynamically added complete/delete buttons, (4) tasks are stored in localStorage and restored on page load, (5) a filter bar (All, Active, Completed) shows/hides tasks using CSS class toggles. No framework, no library — pure DOM manipulation to understand what frameworks abstract away.
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension
Browser DevTools Elements tab for inspecting DOM changes in real time
Clean HTML template with basic styling

review.value: Instead of attaching listeners to every child, attach one listener to the parent and use event.target to identify the clicked child. This handles dynamically added elements (they do not need their own listener) and uses less memory (one listener vs many).

textContent sets plain text (safe). innerHTML parses a string as HTML (dangerous: if the string contains user input, it enables XSS attacks). Always use textContent for user-provided data. Use createElement for dynamic HTML structure.

event.preventDefault() stops the browser's default action: form submission (page reload), link navigation, checkbox toggling. Essential for building SPA-like behavior where JavaScript handles actions that would otherwise trigger full page loads.

Reading a layout property (offsetHeight) after writing to the DOM forces the browser to recalculate layout synchronously. Alternating reads and writes in a loop causes layout thrashing. Batch all reads, then batch all writes, or use requestAnimationFrame.

learning_objectives: Build a REST API using Node.js and Express with proper routing and middleware
Implement CRUD operations with correct HTTP methods and status codes
Handle middleware chains for logging, authentication, error handling, and input validation
Structure an Express project with separation of concerns (routes, controllers, middleware, models)
readings: Express.js Documentation: Getting Started and Guide sections
MDN Web Docs: Express/Node Introduction
Node.js Documentation: HTTP module and Event Loop

lecture_notes.introduction: Most web applications need a backend: a server that manages data, authenticates users, enforces business rules, and serves the API that the frontend consumes. Node.js lets you write server-side code in JavaScript, using the same language across the full stack. Express is the most widely used Node.js web framework, providing a minimal, flexible foundation for building APIs and web applications. Express is built around two core concepts: routing (mapping HTTP requests to handler functions) and middleware (a pipeline of functions that process each request before it reaches the route handler). A typical Express request flows through several middleware layers: a logging middleware records the request method and URL, a body-parsing middleware converts the JSON request body into a JavaScript object, an authentication middleware verifies the JWT token in the Authorization header, a validation middleware checks that required fields are present and have valid types, and finally the route handler processes the request and sends a response. This layered architecture keeps each concern isolated and testable. Understanding Express deeply — how middleware chains work, how error handling middleware catches thrown exceptions, how the request-response cycle completes — is essential for building reliable, maintainable backend services.
key_concepts: Express application: const app = express(); app.listen(port) starts the HTTP server; app.use() registers middleware; app.get/post/put/delete() register route handlers
Routing: app.get('/api/books', handler) matches GET requests to /api/books; app.get('/api/books/:id', handler) captures the id parameter in req.params.id
Middleware: functions receiving (req, res, next); can modify req/res, call next() to pass control, or send a response to end the chain; registered with app.use()
Common middleware: express.json() (parse JSON body), cors() (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing), morgan() (HTTP request logging), helmet() (security headers)
Error handling middleware: (err, req, res, next) with four parameters; Express routes thrown errors and next(err) calls to error middleware for centralized error responses
Input validation: checking request body, params, and query against expected types and constraints; libraries like express-validator or Zod for schema-based validation
REST principles: resource-based URLs (nouns not verbs), proper HTTP methods (GET reads, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH updates, DELETE removes), proper status codes, stateless communication
Project structure: routes/ (URL mapping), controllers/ (request handling logic), middleware/ (reusable middleware), models/ (data layer), config/ (environment and database configuration)
Environment variables: storing secrets (database URLs, API keys) in .env files loaded by dotenv; never committing secrets to version control
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete REST API for a bookstore: (1) Set up Express with body parser, CORS, and Morgan logging. (2) Create CRUD routes: GET /api/books (list), GET /api/books/:id (detail), POST /api/books (create with validation), PUT /api/books/:id (update), DELETE /api/books/:id (delete). (3) Add input validation middleware that rejects requests with missing title or invalid ISBN format. (4) Add centralized error handling middleware that returns consistent JSON error responses. (5) Test every endpoint using Postman/Thunder Client showing proper status codes (200, 201, 400, 404, 500).
setup_requirements: Node.js 20+ and npm installed
Postman, Thunder Client, or curl for API testing
Express, cors, morgan, dotenv packages installed
VS Code with REST Client extension (optional)

review.value: Express processes each request through a chain of middleware functions. Each can modify req/res, call next() to continue, or send a response to end the chain. Order matters: authentication middleware must come before route handlers that require authentication.

URLs represent resources (nouns, not verbs): GET /books (list), POST /books (create), GET /books/:id (read), PUT /books/:id (replace), DELETE /books/:id (delete). Avoid /getBooks, /createBook — the HTTP method conveys the action.

Express recognizes error-handling middleware by its four-parameter signature: (err, req, res, next). Place it after all routes. When route handlers call next(err) or throw, Express skips to the error middleware. Always return a consistent JSON error format.

Route files map URLs to controller functions. Controller functions handle request/response logic. Service functions contain business logic. Model functions handle data access. This separation makes each layer independently testable.

learning_objectives: Design RESTful APIs with proper resource modeling, versioning, and pagination
Implement API documentation using OpenAPI/Swagger specification
Handle API versioning, rate limiting, and HATEOAS principles
Test APIs systematically with automated request collections
readings: Fielding, 'Architectural Styles and the Design of Network-based Software Architectures', Ch. 5 (REST)
OpenAPI Specification 3.0 Documentation
Best Practices for Designing a Pragmatic RESTful API (Vinay Sahni article)

lecture_notes.introduction: REST (Representational State Transfer) is the dominant architectural style for web APIs. Roy Fielding defined REST in his 2000 doctoral dissertation as a set of constraints that, when applied to web services, produce scalable, maintainable, and evolvable systems. In practice, most APIs described as 'RESTful' implement a subset of Fielding's constraints: resource-based URLs, proper HTTP method semantics, stateless communication, and standard media types (JSON). Designing a good REST API requires thinking carefully about resource modeling (what are the nouns in your domain?), URL structure (hierarchical paths that reflect relationships), HTTP method selection (which operation does each method represent?), status code usage (communicating outcomes precisely), pagination (handling large collections efficiently), filtering and sorting (letting clients request exactly the data they need), error response format (consistent structure for machine-readable error handling), and versioning (evolving the API without breaking existing clients). A well-designed API is intuitive to use, self-documenting through consistent conventions, and resilient to change. The OpenAPI specification (formerly Swagger) provides a standard format for documenting APIs that enables automatic generation of client libraries, test suites, and interactive documentation.
key_concepts: Resource modeling: identify the nouns in your domain (books, authors, orders, reviews); each resource has a URL (/api/books, /api/books/42), representations (JSON), and operations (HTTP methods)
URL design: use plural nouns (/books not /book), nest related resources (/authors/7/books), keep URLs shallow (avoid /api/v1/publishers/3/authors/7/books/42/reviews — use /books/42/reviews instead)
Pagination: offset-based (page=2&limit=20) or cursor-based (after=abc123&limit=20); return total count, next/previous links, and current page metadata in the response
Filtering and sorting: query parameters for filtering (GET /books?genre=fiction&year=2024) and sorting (GET /books?sort=-publishedDate for descending); document supported parameters

Error response format: consistent JSON structure with status code, error type, human-readable message, and field-level validation details; example: { error: 'validation_error', message: 'Invalid input', details: [{ field: 'isbn', issue: 'must be 13 digits' }] }

API versioning: URL path versioning (/api/v1/books), header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v1+json), or query parameter versioning (?version=1); URL versioning is most common and easiest to implement
Rate limiting: limiting API requests per client per time window (100 requests/minute); return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header; implemented via middleware with Redis counter
OpenAPI/Swagger: YAML/JSON specification describing endpoints, parameters, request/response bodies, and authentication; generates interactive documentation (Swagger UI), client SDKs, and test stubs
CORS for APIs: Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers; preflight OPTIONS requests for non-simple cross-origin requests
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design and document a REST API for a library management system: (1) Model resources (books, members, loans, reservations) with their relationships. (2) Define endpoints with proper HTTP methods, URL patterns, request/response bodies, and status codes. (3) Implement pagination for GET /api/books with offset-based pagination including total_count, page, and limit in the response. (4) Add filtering (GET /api/books?author=Tolkien&available=true). (5) Write an OpenAPI 3.0 specification and serve interactive Swagger UI documentation at /api/docs.
setup_requirements: Express application from previous session
swagger-ui-express and swagger-jsdoc packages
Postman collection for testing all endpoints
OpenAPI 3.0 YAML template

review.value: Never return unbounded collections. An endpoint that returns all 100,000 books in one response will crash clients and overload the server. Always paginate list endpoints and include metadata (total_count, next_page, has_more).

Every API error response should use the same JSON structure regardless of the error type. Clients should be able to parse any error response with one code path. Include enough detail for debugging without leaking internal implementation details.

URL versioning (/api/v1/books) is the simplest approach and the most visible to consumers. Major version changes that break compatibility get a new version. Minor additive changes (new fields, new endpoints) are backward-compatible and do not need a new version.

An OpenAPI specification is a machine-readable API contract. It generates interactive docs (Swagger UI), client SDKs, mock servers for frontend development, and automated contract tests. Write the spec before or alongside the implementation.

learning_objectives: Design relational database schemas with proper normalization and relationships
Write SQL queries for CRUD operations, joins, aggregation, and transactions
Use MongoDB for document-oriented data storage with appropriate data modeling
Choose between SQL and NoSQL databases based on data characteristics and access patterns
readings: PostgreSQL Documentation: Tutorial (Creating Tables, Querying, Joins)
MongoDB Documentation: Introduction and CRUD Operations
Kleppmann, Designing Data-Intensive Applications, Ch. 2 (Data Models and Query Languages)

lecture_notes.introduction: Almost every web application needs persistent data storage. The choice of database shapes how you model data, how you query it, how it scales, and what consistency guarantees you get. Relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL) store data in tables with rows and columns, enforce schemas, support complex queries with SQL (Structured Query Language), and provide ACID transactions for data integrity. They excel when data has clear relationships (users have orders, orders contain items, items belong to categories) and when you need complex queries that join multiple tables. Document databases (MongoDB) store data as flexible JSON-like documents in collections, do not enforce a fixed schema, and excel when data is naturally hierarchical or when the schema changes frequently. A blog post with its comments, tags, and author information maps naturally to a single MongoDB document, while the same data in a relational database requires four tables and joins. Neither approach is universally better: relational databases provide stronger consistency and more powerful querying, while document databases provide flexibility and horizontal scalability for specific workloads. Most web applications benefit from understanding both, and many use both: a relational database for transactional data (orders, payments) and a document database for content or analytics.
key_concepts: Relational schema design: tables, columns with types, primary keys, foreign keys, indexes; normalization to eliminate redundancy (1NF, 2NF, 3NF); denormalization for read performance when justified
SQL CRUD: INSERT INTO, SELECT (with WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT), UPDATE, DELETE; parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
SQL joins: INNER JOIN (matching rows in both tables), LEFT JOIN (all rows from left table, matching from right), many-to-many relationships via junction tables
SQL aggregation: GROUP BY, COUNT, SUM, AVG, HAVING for filtered aggregation; useful for reports and dashboards
Transactions: BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK; ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) ensure data integrity when multiple operations must succeed or fail together
MongoDB documents: JSON-like BSON format, flexible schema (documents in the same collection can have different fields), nested objects and arrays for hierarchical data
MongoDB CRUD: insertOne/insertMany, find (with query filters and projections), updateOne/updateMany, deleteOne/deleteMany; the aggregation pipeline for complex queries

SQL vs NoSQL trade-offs: SQL for strong consistency, complex queries, and relational data; NoSQL for schema flexibility, horizontal scaling, and hierarchical data; consider access patterns, consistency requirements, and team expertise

ORMs and ODMs: Sequelize/Prisma (SQL), Mongoose (MongoDB) — object-relational/document mappers that abstract database operations into language-native objects; trade-off between convenience and query control
Database migrations: versioned schema changes (add column, create table, modify index) applied in order; tools like Prisma Migrate, Knex, or Flyway; always reversible for safe rollback
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build the data layer for the bookstore API: (1) Design a PostgreSQL schema with books, authors, and book_authors (many-to-many) tables with proper types, constraints, and indexes. (2) Write SQL queries: find books by author (join), count books per genre (aggregation), create an order with multiple books as a transaction. (3) Implement the same data in MongoDB: a books collection with embedded author array. (4) Compare the query complexity and flexibility of each approach. (5) Connect the Express API to PostgreSQL using Prisma ORM and demonstrate a complete request flow from HTTP request to database query to JSON response.
setup_requirements: PostgreSQL installed locally or via Docker (docker run -d -p 5432:5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=dev postgres)
MongoDB installed locally or via Docker (docker run -d -p 27017:27017 mongo)
Prisma ORM or Knex.js for SQL, Mongoose for MongoDB
Database GUI tool (pgAdmin, TablePlus, or MongoDB Compass)

review.value: Never concatenate user input into SQL strings. Always use parameterized queries: db.query('SELECT * FROM books WHERE id = $1', [id]). ORMs handle this automatically, but understanding the vulnerability is essential.

Normalization eliminates redundancy (single source of truth for each fact). Denormalization duplicates data for read performance (embedding author name in every book row avoids a join). Normalize by default; denormalize only when query performance requires it and you accept the update complexity.

MongoDB excels for: content management (blog posts with variable structure), real-time analytics (flexible event schemas), catalogs (products with different attribute sets), and prototyping (schema changes without migrations). Use SQL for anything transactional or highly relational.

Never modify a production database schema manually. Use migration files that are version-controlled, reviewed, and applied automatically. Every schema change is a migration: add column, create index, rename table. Migrations must be reversible.

learning_objectives: Build React applications using functional components with hooks
Manage component state with useState and derive computed values from state
Handle side effects (data fetching, subscriptions, timers) with useEffect
Compose component hierarchies with props for parent-to-child data flow
readings: React Documentation: Quick Start and Thinking in React
React Documentation: useState, useEffect, and Hooks reference
Overreacted Blog (Dan Abramov): 'A Complete Guide to useEffect'

lecture_notes.introduction: React changed how we build web user interfaces by introducing a component model where the UI is a pure function of state. Instead of manually manipulating the DOM when data changes (the jQuery approach: find the element, update its text, add a class, remove a child), you declare what the UI should look like for each possible state, and React computes the minimum DOM updates necessary to match. This declarative approach eliminates an entire class of bugs: you never forget to update a part of the UI because React updates everything that depends on the changed state automatically. A React application is a tree of components, each responsible for rendering a piece of the UI. Components receive data from their parents via props (immutable inputs) and manage their own local state via useState. When state changes, the component re-renders, and React efficiently updates only the DOM elements that actually changed. useEffect handles side effects that do not fit the declarative rendering model: fetching data from an API, subscribing to a WebSocket, setting a document title, or starting a timer. The mental model for useEffect is synchronization: 'keep this side effect synchronized with this state.' When the dependencies change, the effect runs again. This is a shift from the lifecycle thinking of class components (componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmount) to a more declarative model.
key_concepts: Components: reusable, composable UI building blocks; each component is a function that receives props and returns JSX (React elements describing what to render)

JSX: JavaScript syntax extension that looks like HTML but compiles to React.createElement calls; expressions in curly braces {}, conditional rendering with && and ternary, list rendering with .map() and key prop
Props: data passed from parent to child; immutable within the child; the primary mechanism for component communication; destructure in the function signature for clarity
useState: const [count, setCount] = useState(0); creates a state variable and its setter; calling the setter triggers a re-render; state updates may be batched and are asynchronous
useEffect: useEffect(() => { /* effect */ return () => { /* cleanup */ } }, [dependencies]); runs after render when dependencies change; cleanup runs before re-running and on unmount
Dependency arrays: [] means run once on mount, [dep] means run when dep changes, no array means run after every render; exhaustive deps lint rule prevents stale closure bugs
Component composition: building complex UIs by nesting simple components; children prop for wrapper components; render props and compound components for advanced patterns
Lifting state up: when two sibling components need to share state, move the state to their closest common ancestor and pass it down via props; the ancestor becomes the single source of truth
Immutable state updates: never mutate state directly (state.push is wrong); create new arrays/objects: setItems([...items, newItem]), setUser({...user, name: 'new'});  spread operator is essential
React DevTools: inspect component tree, view props and state, track renders, profile performance; essential for debugging React applications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a weather dashboard from scratch: (1) Create a SearchBar component with controlled input (useState for the query). (2) Create a WeatherCard component that receives weather data via props and displays temperature, conditions, and an icon. (3) Create a ForecastList component that renders multiple WeatherCards. (4) In the App component, use useState for the city and weather data, useEffect to fetch weather from OpenWeatherMap API when the city changes, and handle loading and error states. (5) Lift state up: when SearchBar submits, it calls a callback prop that updates the city in App, which triggers the useEffect to fetch new weather data.
setup_requirements: Node.js 20+ installed
Vite for project scaffolding: npm create vite@latest -- --template react
React DevTools browser extension installed
OpenWeatherMap API key (free tier) or mock data service

review.value: In React, you describe what the UI should look like for each state, not how to transition between states. React computes the minimal DOM updates needed. This eliminates bugs where the DOM gets out of sync with the application state.

Hooks must be called at the top level of a component (not inside loops, conditions, or nested functions) and only inside React function components or custom hooks. These rules ensure React can correctly associate hook calls with components across re-renders.

The dependency array tells React when to re-run the effect. Missing a dependency causes stale closures (the effect uses an old value). Including unnecessary dependencies causes excessive re-runs. The eslint-plugin-react-hooks exhaustive-deps rule catches these bugs.

A controlled input has its value driven by React state: value={query} onChange={e => setQuery(e.target.value)}. React is the single source of truth. Uncontrolled inputs manage their own state internally, which is simpler but harder to synchronize with the rest of the application.

learning_objectives: Manage complex application state with useReducer and Context API
Implement client-side routing with React Router for multi-page applications
Create custom hooks to encapsulate and reuse stateful logic
Evaluate when to use local state, context, or external state management libraries
readings: React Documentation: useReducer, useContext, and custom hooks
React Router v6 Documentation: Quick Start and nested routes
Kent C. Dodds: 'Application State Management with React'

lecture_notes.introduction: As React applications grow beyond a few components, two challenges emerge: state management and navigation. State management becomes complex when multiple components across different parts of the component tree need access to the same data (the current user, a shopping cart, theme preferences). Lifting state up works for small applications, but passing props through many intermediate components ('prop drilling') creates fragile, tightly-coupled component hierarchies. The Context API solves this by providing a way to share values across the component tree without passing props at every level. useReducer complements Context for complex state logic: instead of multiple useState calls and scattered update logic, a reducer centralizes all state transitions in a single function, making state changes predictable, debuggable, and testable. Client-side routing is the second challenge: single-page applications need to map URLs to components, handle navigation without full page reloads, support the browser back button, and manage route parameters. React Router v6 provides a declarative, component-based routing system that integrates naturally with React's component model. Routes are components, links are components, and navigation is a side effect that React Router manages. Custom hooks are the mechanism for extracting and reusing stateful logic: a useAuth hook that checks login status, a useLocalStorage hook that persists state to localStorage, a useFetch hook that manages loading and error states for any API call.
key_concepts: useReducer: const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState); reducer is a pure function (state, action) => newState; centralizes state transitions, better for complex state with multiple sub-values
Context API: createContext(), Provider wraps the tree with a value, useContext() consumes the value in any descendant; avoids prop drilling for widely-shared state
Context + useReducer pattern: Provider holds the reducer state and dispatch; consumers access state and dispatch actions; mimics Redux without external dependencies
Custom hooks: functions starting with 'use' that encapsulate stateful logic; useAuth(), useLocalStorage(key, initial), useFetch(url); composable and testable independently of UI
React Router v6: BrowserRouter wraps the app, Routes contains Route elements, Route maps a path to a component; Link for navigation, useNavigate for programmatic navigation
Nested routes: parent Route with child Routes renders the parent layout with an Outlet for the child; enables shared layouts (sidebar, header) across related pages
Route parameters: /books/:id captures the id in useParams(); /books?search=react captured with useSearchParams(); dynamic routes for detail pages
Protected routes: a wrapper component that checks authentication state and redirects unauthenticated users to /login; combines Context (auth state) with React Router (redirect)

State management spectrum: local state (useState) for component-specific data, lifted state for sibling sharing, Context for app-wide data (auth, theme), external libraries (Redux, Zustand) for very complex state
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a multi-page bookstore application: (1) Set up React Router with routes for Home (/), Book List (/books), Book Detail (/books/:id), Cart (/cart), and Login (/login). (2) Create a shared Layout component with navigation header and Outlet for page content (nested routes). (3) Implement a cart using useReducer (actions: ADD_ITEM, REMOVE_ITEM, UPDATE_QUANTITY, CLEAR_CART) wrapped in CartContext so any component can add to cart without prop drilling. (4) Create a useAuth custom hook that manages login state and provides login/logout functions via AuthContext. (5) Protect the /cart route so unauthenticated users are redirected to /login.
setup_requirements: Vite React project from previous session
react-router-dom v6 installed
React DevTools for inspecting context and state
Mock API data or JSON Server for book data

review.value: Use useState for simple, independent state values (a toggle, a text input). Use useReducer when state has multiple sub-values that change together (a form with many fields), when the next state depends on the previous state, or when state transitions have complex logic.

Context is a dependency injection mechanism, not a state management library. Every context value change re-renders every consumer. For high-frequency updates (mouse position, typing), Context causes performance problems. Use Context for low-frequency, widely-shared state (auth, theme).

Custom hooks must start with 'use' so React can enforce the Rules of Hooks. Name them for what they do: useAuth, useLocalStorage, useFetch, useDebounce. They are the primary code reuse mechanism in React — prefer custom hooks over render props or higher-order components.

Nested routes enable shared layouts: a DashboardLayout route with nested child routes (/dashboard/analytics, /dashboard/settings) shares the sidebar and header. The parent renders an Outlet where the matched child route appears. This eliminates layout duplication.

learning_objectives: Implement user registration and login with password hashing using bcrypt
Issue and verify JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for stateless session management
Protect API routes with authentication middleware and implement role-based access control
Secure the full-stack authentication flow from React login form to Express API to database
readings: JWT.io: Introduction to JSON Web Tokens
OWASP: Authentication Cheat Sheet
Auth0 Blog: 'The Complete Guide to Node.js User Authentication with Auth0'

lecture_notes.introduction: Authentication (verifying who a user is) and authorization (determining what they are allowed to do) are critical for any web application that handles user data. Getting authentication wrong has severe consequences: data breaches, unauthorized access, and regulatory penalties. The standard approach for modern web applications is token-based authentication using JSON Web Tokens (JWTs). The flow: a user submits their email and password to the login endpoint; the server verifies the password against the stored hash (never store plaintext passwords); if valid, the server generates a JWT containing the user's ID and roles, signs it with a secret key, and returns it to the client; the client stores the JWT (in httpOnly cookies or memory, not localStorage for security) and sends it with every subsequent request in the Authorization header; the server middleware verifies the JWT signature and extracts the user identity without a database lookup. This is stateless: the server does not need to store session data, which simplifies horizontal scaling. Authorization adds a second layer: after authentication confirms who the user is, authorization checks whether they have permission to perform the requested action. Role-based access control (RBAC) assigns roles (admin, editor, viewer) to users and checks the role before granting access to protected resources.
key_concepts: Password hashing: bcrypt hashes passwords with a random salt and configurable cost factor; comparison: bcrypt.compare(password, hash); never store plaintext passwords; bcrypt's deliberate slowness defeats brute-force attacks

JWT structure: header (algorithm and type), payload (claims: sub for user ID, exp for expiration, iat for issued-at, custom claims like role), signature (HMAC-SHA256 of header+payload using server secret)

JWT flow: client sends credentials -> server validates -> server signs JWT -> client stores token -> client sends token in Authorization: Bearer <token> header -> server middleware verifies signature and extracts claims

Token storage: httpOnly cookies (server sets the cookie, browser sends it automatically, JavaScript cannot access it — protects against XSS) vs in-memory variable (lost on page refresh but secure) vs localStorage (persistent but vulnerable to XSS)
Authentication middleware: extracts token from Authorization header, verifies signature with jwt.verify(), attaches user to req.user, calls next(); returns 401 if token is missing or invalid
Role-based access control: middleware that checks req.user.role against allowed roles for the route; returns 403 Forbidden if the user lacks the required role

Token expiration and refresh: short-lived access tokens (15 minutes) minimize damage from stolen tokens; refresh tokens (7 days) stored in httpOnly cookies allow obtaining new access tokens without re-login
Common vulnerabilities: storing passwords in plaintext, JWTs in localStorage (XSS), using weak JWT secrets, not validating token expiration, not invalidating tokens on password change

OAuth 2.0 overview: delegated authorization for third-party login (Sign in with Google); authorization code flow, access tokens, and scopes; simplifies registration but adds dependency on external providers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement full-stack authentication for the bookstore: (1) User registration endpoint: hash password with bcrypt (12 rounds), store in database. (2) Login endpoint: verify password with bcrypt.compare, generate JWT with user ID and role, return in httpOnly cookie. (3) Authentication middleware: verify JWT from cookie, attach user to req.user. (4) Protected route: POST /api/books requires authentication. (5) Admin-only route: DELETE /api/books/:id requires admin role. (6) React login form: POST credentials, store authentication state in AuthContext, redirect on success. (7) React Axios interceptor: automatically include credentials (cookies) with every request.
setup_requirements: bcryptjs and jsonwebtoken npm packages installed
Express application with database connection from previous sessions
React application with AuthContext from previous session
Cookie-parser middleware for Express

review.value: JWTs are stateless (server verifies the signature without database lookup); sessions are stateful (server stores session data, usually in Redis). JWTs scale horizontally more easily; sessions are easier to invalidate (just delete the session). Many production systems use both: JWTs for API authentication, sessions for web application login.

Storing JWTs in httpOnly cookies protects against XSS attacks because JavaScript cannot read or modify httpOnly cookies. The browser automatically sends the cookie with every request to the issuing domain. Combine with Secure (HTTPS only) and SameSite=Strict (CSRF protection) flags.

bcrypt's cost factor (rounds) controls how slow hashing is. Higher cost means slower brute-force attacks but slower login. Use a cost factor that takes about 250ms on your server hardware (typically 10-12 rounds). Adjust upward as hardware gets faster.

Use established libraries (bcrypt for hashing, jsonwebtoken for JWTs) and well-known protocols (OAuth 2.0). Cryptographic security depends on subtle implementation details that are easy to get wrong. Custom authentication schemes almost always have vulnerabilities.

learning_objectives: Write unit tests for React components using React Testing Library
Test Express API endpoints with integration tests using Supertest
Apply the testing trophy strategy: mostly integration tests, some unit, few E2E
Test user interactions, async behavior, and error states in React components
readings: React Testing Library Documentation: Introduction and Examples
Kent C. Dodds: 'Testing Implementation Details' and 'Write tests, not too many, mostly integration'
Supertest Documentation: Testing Express APIs

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing web applications presents unique challenges across the frontend-backend boundary. Frontend tests must verify that components render correctly, respond to user interactions, handle asynchronous data fetching, and display error states. Backend tests must verify that API endpoints accept valid input, reject invalid input, return correct status codes, and interact with the database correctly. React Testing Library, created by Kent C. Dodds, embodies the philosophy that tests should resemble how users interact with your application. Instead of testing implementation details (which hook was called, what the internal state is), you test behavior: render the component, find elements the way a user would (by role, label, or text content), interact with them (click, type), and assert on what the user sees (text appears, element is present, error message shows). This approach produces tests that are resilient to refactoring: you can completely rewrite a component's internal implementation, and as long as the user-facing behavior is the same, the tests still pass. For the backend, Supertest sends real HTTP requests to your Express app and asserts on the response status, headers, and body. Combined with a test database that is reset between tests, this gives you high confidence that your API works correctly end to end.
key_concepts: React Testing Library: render(Component), screen.getByRole/getByText/getByLabelText for finding elements, userEvent.click/userEvent.type for interactions, waitFor for async assertions
Testing philosophy: test behavior, not implementation; if the user cannot observe it, do not test it; tests should not break when you refactor internals
Component test structure: Arrange (render with props and context), Act (simulate user interaction), Assert (verify visible output); follows the AAA pattern
Testing async behavior: waitFor(() => expect(screen.getByText('loaded data')).toBeInTheDocument()); handles useEffect data fetching by waiting for the DOM to update
Mocking API calls: mock fetch or axios at the module level (jest.mock or msw — Mock Service Worker) to return controlled responses; test loading, success, and error states

Supertest for Express: request(app).get('/api/books').expect(200).expect('Content-Type', /json/).then(res => expect(res.body).toHaveLength(5)); sends real HTTP requests to the Express app without starting a server
Test database: use a separate test database that is reset (truncated or recreated) before each test suite; ensures test isolation; Docker makes spinning up test databases trivial

Testing trophy: static analysis (linting) at the base, unit tests, integration tests (the largest layer), and E2E tests at the top; Kent C. Dodds' variation of the testing pyramid emphasizing integration tests

Mock Service Worker (MSW): intercepts network requests at the service worker level, allowing you to mock API responses without modifying application code; shared mock definitions between tests and development

Coverage and quality: code coverage measures execution, not correctness; a line can be covered but not asserted. Focus on meaningful assertions that would fail if behavior changed, not on coverage percentage.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Test the bookstore application: (1) Unit test the WeatherCard component: render with props, verify temperature and conditions are displayed, verify icon is present. (2) Test the SearchBar component: render, type a city name with userEvent.type, click search, verify the onSearch callback was called with the correct value. (3) Test the BookList page with async data: mock the fetch call to return test books, render the component, wait for loading to disappear, verify book titles are displayed, test the error state by mocking a failed fetch. (4) Integration test the Express books API: POST /api/books with valid data expects 201, POST with missing title expects 400 with error message, GET /api/books expects 200 with array, GET /api/books/999 expects 404.
setup_requirements: Vitest or Jest configured with React Testing Library
@testing-library/react, @testing-library/user-event, @testing-library/jest-dom installed
Supertest installed for Express API testing
msw (Mock Service Worker) for API mocking in frontend tests

review.value: Find elements the way users find them: by role (button, textbox, heading), by label text, by displayed text. Avoid finding by test-id or CSS class unless there is no accessible alternative. This also catches accessibility issues.

When a component fetches data in useEffect, the initial render shows a loading state. Use waitFor(() => expect(screen.getByText('Book Title')).toBeInTheDocument()) to wait for the async operation to complete and the DOM to update.

Supertest sends real HTTP requests through your Express middleware chain, exercising routing, middleware, validation, and error handling. These tests catch integration bugs that unit-testing individual middleware functions would miss.

MSW intercepts fetch/XMLHttpRequest at the network level, returning mock responses. Unlike mocking fetch directly, MSW tests the actual fetch call your code makes. Mock definitions can be shared between tests and development for consistent behavior.

learning_objectives: Deploy frontend applications to static hosting platforms (Vercel, Netlify)
Deploy backend applications to cloud platforms (Render, Railway, Fly.io)
Configure environment variables, HTTPS, custom domains, and deployment pipelines
Set up CI/CD for automatic deployment on push to main
readings: Vercel Documentation: Deploying a Vite React App
Render Documentation: Deploying a Node.js Application
12factor.net: The Twelve-Factor App (Config, Processes, Port Binding sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Deployment is where your application becomes real. A web application running on localhost is a development artifact; a web application deployed to a public URL is a product. Deployment involves several concerns beyond uploading files: environment configuration (database URLs, API keys, feature flags must differ between development, staging, and production), HTTPS (all production traffic must be encrypted, which requires TLS certificates), domain configuration (DNS records pointing your domain to the hosting platform), continuous deployment (automatically deploying when new code is pushed to the main branch), monitoring (knowing when your deployed application is down or slow), and cost management (understanding the pricing model of your hosting provider to avoid surprise bills). Modern platforms have dramatically simplified deployment. Vercel and Netlify handle frontend deployment (React, Next.js) with zero configuration: connect your GitHub repository, and every push to main triggers a build and deployment. Render and Railway handle backend deployment similarly: specify your build command and start command, connect your database, and every push deploys. But simplicity should not breed ignorance: understanding what these platforms do under the hood — building Docker containers, configuring reverse proxies, provisioning TLS certificates, managing rolling deployments — enables you to debug deployment issues and make informed architectural decisions.
key_concepts: Static hosting (frontend): Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages; the build step produces static files (HTML, CSS, JS, assets) that are served from a CDN; no server-side code runs at request time

Platform-as-a-Service (backend): Render, Railway, Fly.io, Heroku; the platform builds your application (from Dockerfile or detected runtime), starts it on a managed server, routes traffic, and handles scaling
Environment variables: VITE_API_URL for frontend (embedded at build time), DATABASE_URL for backend (available at runtime); never hardcode secrets; use the hosting platform's environment variable UI
HTTPS and TLS: all production traffic must be encrypted; hosting platforms provision TLS certificates automatically via Let's Encrypt; custom domains require DNS configuration (CNAME or A record)

CI/CD pipeline: GitHub Actions triggers on push to main, runs tests, builds the application, and deploys to the hosting platform; deployment fails if tests fail, preventing broken code from reaching production

Build vs runtime: frontend environment variables (VITE_*) are embedded during the build step and cannot change without rebuilding; backend environment variables are read at startup and can be changed by restarting the process

Database hosting: managed PostgreSQL (Render, Neon, Supabase), managed MongoDB (Atlas); connection strings in environment variables; ensure the database is in the same region as the application server for low latency
Rolling deployments: the platform deploys the new version alongside the old one, routes traffic to the new version after health checks pass, then stops the old version; zero-downtime deployments

Preview deployments: platforms like Vercel deploy every PR to a unique URL for testing before merge; enables QA review, stakeholder feedback, and visual regression testing on the actual deployed build
Cost awareness: free tiers have limits (Render: 750 hours/month, Vercel: 100GB bandwidth/month); understand what triggers billing (build minutes, bandwidth, database storage, persistent storage)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy the bookstore application: (1) Deploy the React frontend to Vercel: connect GitHub repo, configure build settings (npm run build, dist/ output), set VITE_API_URL environment variable pointing to the backend URL. (2) Deploy the Express backend to Render: connect GitHub repo, set build and start commands, configure DATABASE_URL and JWT_SECRET environment variables. (3) Provision a managed PostgreSQL database on Render and connect it to the backend. (4) Configure a custom domain with DNS CNAME record. (5) Set up GitHub Actions CI: on push to main, run tests; if tests pass, Vercel and Render automatically deploy. (6) Make a code change, push to main, and watch the full CI/CD pipeline execute.
setup_requirements: Vercel account connected to GitHub
Render account connected to GitHub
GitHub repository with frontend and backend code
Custom domain (optional, .dev or .app domain recommended)
GitHub Actions workflow file

review.value: Secrets, API URLs, and configuration that differs between environments must be in environment variables, never in code. The same codebase should be deployable to development, staging, and production by changing only environment variables.

Every PR deployed to a unique URL enables QA testing, stakeholder review, and automated visual regression testing before merge. This catches deployment issues (missing environment variables, build failures) before they affect production.

Static frontend files (HTML, CSS, JS) are served from a CDN (Content Delivery Network) with edge nodes worldwide. Users download from the nearest edge, reducing latency dramatically compared to serving from a single origin server.

Rolling deployments keep the old version running while the new version starts and passes health checks. Traffic is gradually shifted to the new version. If the new version fails health checks, the old version continues serving — no downtime.

learning_objectives: Measure web application performance using Core Web Vitals and Lighthouse
Optimize frontend performance with code splitting, lazy loading, and caching
Optimize backend performance with database indexing, query optimization, and response caching
Apply performance budgets to prevent performance regressions
readings: web.dev: Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS)
MDN Web Docs: Lazy loading, Code splitting
High Performance Browser Networking (Ilya Grigorik), Ch. 10-12

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance is a feature. A page that takes 5 seconds to load loses 90% of its visitors. Google uses Core Web Vitals as a ranking signal: slow sites rank lower in search results. Performance optimization requires measurement before action: you must identify the bottleneck before trying to fix it, because optimizing the wrong thing wastes effort and can make the real bottleneck harder to find. Core Web Vitals measure the three aspects of page load experience that users care about most: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP, how quickly the main content appears), First Input Delay (FID, how quickly the page responds to the first user interaction, being replaced by Interaction to Next Paint), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS, how much the page layout shifts unexpectedly during load). Lighthouse provides automated performance audits with specific recommendations. On the frontend, the most impactful optimizations are reducing JavaScript bundle size (code splitting, tree shaking, lazy loading routes), optimizing images (compression, responsive sizes, modern formats like WebP/AVIF), and leveraging browser caching (Cache-Control headers, service workers). On the backend, the most impactful optimizations are database query optimization (indexes, query analysis, avoiding N+1 queries), response caching (Redis for frequently accessed data, HTTP cache headers for static responses), and efficient API design (pagination, field selection, avoiding over-fetching). Performance budgets set limits on bundle size, load time, and other metrics that trigger alerts when exceeded, preventing gradual performance degradation.
key_concepts: Core Web Vitals: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint, target <2.5s), INP (Interaction to Next Paint, target <200ms), CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift, target <0.1); measured in the field (real users) and lab (Lighthouse)
Lighthouse: automated performance audit that scores performance, accessibility, best practices, and SEO; provides specific actionable recommendations with estimated impact
Code splitting: React.lazy() and Suspense for route-based splitting; only load the JavaScript needed for the current page; reduces initial bundle size dramatically for large applications
Image optimization: compress images (lossy for photos, lossless for graphics), use modern formats (WebP, AVIF), serve responsive sizes (srcset), lazy-load below-the-fold images (loading='lazy')

Caching strategy: Cache-Control headers for static assets (max-age=31536000 for hashed filenames), ETag for dynamic content, Redis for server-side query result caching, service workers for offline support
Database indexing: create indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses; explain queries to verify index usage; indexes speed reads but slow writes — index judiciously
N+1 query problem: fetching a list of books, then fetching the author for each book individually (1 + N queries); solve with a JOIN query or eager loading in the ORM (include/populate)
Bundle analysis: tools like vite-plugin-visualizer or webpack-bundle-analyzer show which libraries consume the most space; replace large libraries with smaller alternatives (date-fns vs moment.js)
Performance budgets: set maximum bundle size (e.g., 200KB gzipped JavaScript), maximum LCP (2.5s), minimum Lighthouse score (90); enforce in CI to prevent regressions
Content Delivery Network (CDN): serve static assets from edge nodes near users; reduces latency from hundreds of milliseconds to single digits for cached content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Optimize the bookstore application: (1) Run Lighthouse audit and record baseline scores. (2) Add code splitting: lazy-load the BookDetail and Cart routes with React.lazy and Suspense. (3) Optimize images: convert to WebP, add srcset for responsive sizes, add loading='lazy' for below-fold images. (4) Fix CLS: add explicit width/height to images, add skeleton loaders for async content. (5) Backend: add a database index on books.genre and books.published_date, demonstrate query time improvement with EXPLAIN ANALYZE. (6) Add Redis caching for GET /api/books (cache for 60 seconds, invalidate on POST/PUT/DELETE). (7) Re-run Lighthouse and compare before/after scores.
setup_requirements: Chrome Lighthouse (built into DevTools)
vite-plugin-visualizer for bundle analysis
Redis running locally (docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis) for backend caching
WebP/AVIF image conversion tool (sharp or squoosh.app)

review.value: Always measure performance before and after optimizing. Without measurement, you might optimize something that is not the bottleneck, wasting effort. Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools Performance tab, and server-side profiling identify the real bottlenecks.

A typical React app loads all route code upfront. Code splitting with React.lazy loads only the current route's code, loading other routes on demand. This can reduce initial bundle size by 50-80% for large applications.

If an API endpoint makes one query for a list and then N queries for related data, it has an N+1 problem. With 100 items, that is 101 queries. A single JOIN query replaces all 101 queries. ORMs often cause N+1 queries when you access related data without eager loading.

Performance degrades gradually when there is no enforcement. A performance budget in CI fails the build when bundle size exceeds the limit or Lighthouse score drops below the threshold. This catches regressions before they reach production.

learning_objectives: Build accessible web applications that conform to WCAG 2.1 AA guidelines
Use ARIA attributes correctly for interactive components that lack native semantics
Implement technical SEO best practices for search engine visibility
Test accessibility with automated tools and screen reader testing
readings: MDN Web Docs: Accessibility (A11y) Guide
WCAG 2.1: Understanding the Guidelines (W3C)
Google Search Central: SEO Starter Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessibility is not optional, and it is not a feature you add at the end. It is a fundamental quality of well-built web applications. Approximately 15% of the world's population has some form of disability that affects how they use the web: visual impairments (blindness, low vision, color blindness), motor impairments (inability to use a mouse), auditory impairments (deafness), and cognitive impairments (dyslexia, attention disorders). Accessible web applications work for all these users through semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, screen reader support, sufficient color contrast, clear language, and respect for user preferences (reduced motion, high contrast, text scaling). Beyond the ethical imperative, accessibility has practical benefits: accessible sites are also more SEO-friendly (search engines are essentially blind users that parse your HTML for meaning), more usable on mobile devices (touch targets, readable text), and legally required in many jurisdictions (ADA in the US, EN 301 549 in the EU). WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) 2.1 provides the international standard, with four principles: Perceivable (users can perceive the content), Operable (users can interact with the interface), Understandable (users can understand the content and interface), and Robust (content works with assistive technologies). Level AA conformance is the standard target for most web applications. SEO (Search Engine Optimization) determines whether your application appears in search results. Technical SEO — semantic HTML, meta tags, structured data, performance, mobile-friendliness — is largely the same set of practices that make a site accessible.
key_concepts: Semantic HTML for accessibility: use <button> for clickable actions (not <div onclick>), <a> for navigation, <label> associated with <input>, <nav> for navigation, <main> for primary content; semantics enable screen readers and keyboard navigation
Keyboard navigation: all interactive elements must be keyboard-accessible (Tab to focus, Enter/Space to activate, Escape to close); manage focus order with tabindex; trap focus in modal dialogs

ARIA attributes: aria-label (accessible name for elements without visible text), aria-describedby (additional description), aria-expanded (toggle state), aria-live (announce dynamic content changes), role (when semantic HTML is insufficient)
Color contrast: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text, 3:1 for large text; do not rely on color alone to convey information (add icons, patterns, or text labels)

Screen reader testing: use VoiceOver (Mac), NVDA (Windows), or ChromeVox (browser extension) to navigate your application without seeing the screen; reveals missing labels, poor heading structure, and inaccessible components

Alt text: descriptive for informative images (alt='Bar chart showing revenue growth from $1M to $5M over 2024'), empty for decorative images (alt=''); screen readers skip empty alt but announce missing alt as the filename

Meta tags for SEO: <title> (unique per page, under 60 characters), <meta name='description'> (page summary, under 155 characters), <meta name='viewport'> (mobile rendering), <link rel='canonical'> (preferred URL)

Structured data: JSON-LD markup that tells search engines the meaning of your content (product with price, article with author and date, FAQ with questions and answers); enables rich results in Google
Technical SEO: fast page load (Core Web Vitals), mobile-friendly layout, valid HTML, descriptive URLs (/books/the-great-gatsby vs /books/12345), proper heading hierarchy, sitemap.xml, robots.txt

axe-core: automated accessibility testing library that catches approximately 57% of WCAG violations automatically; run in CI with jest-axe or as a browser extension; complement with manual screen reader testing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit and fix the bookstore application for accessibility and SEO: (1) Run axe DevTools on the homepage and identify violations (missing alt text, insufficient contrast, form labels not associated). (2) Fix each violation: add alt text, increase button contrast ratio, associate labels with inputs using htmlFor/id. (3) Add ARIA: aria-expanded on the mobile menu toggle, aria-live='polite' on the cart item count, role='search' on the search form. (4) Test with VoiceOver: navigate the entire application using only keyboard and screen reader, fix any discovered issues. (5) Add SEO: unique titles and descriptions for each page using React Helmet, JSON-LD structured data for books, sitemap generation. (6) Re-run Lighthouse and verify accessibility and SEO scores above 90.

VoiceOver (Mac) or NVDA (Windows) screen reader
Contrast checking tool (WebAIM Contrast Checker or built into DevTools)

review.value: Always use the native HTML element before reaching for ARIA. A <button> is accessible by default (keyboard navigable, screen reader announced, click handler works with Enter/Space). A <div role='button' tabindex='0' onkeypress=...> replicates what <button> provides for free.

The first rule of ARIA: do not use ARIA if a native HTML element provides the same semantics. ARIA is for complex custom components (tabs, comboboxes, tree views) where no native HTML element exists. Incorrect ARIA is worse than no ARIA.

Good alt text describes the content and function of an image. 'Photo' is useless. 'Golden retriever puppy sitting on a red couch' is descriptive. For functional images (icons as buttons), describe the action: 'Close dialog' not 'X icon'.

Search engines are blind users. They parse HTML for meaning, follow links, read alt text, and use heading structure. An accessible site is inherently more SEO-friendly. Improving accessibility and SEO are largely the same work.
