lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together everything we have covered across the semester: HTML structure, CSS layout, JavaScript interactivity, React component architecture, Node.js and Express APIs, database design, authentication, testing, deployment, performance, and accessibility. Building a full-stack web application from scratch exercises all of these skills in combination, revealing how they reinforce each other and where the real complexity lies (not in any single technology, but in the integration between them). Your final project presentation is your opportunity to demonstrate not just what you built, but why you built it the way you did. Every web application involves hundreds of decisions: why React instead of vanilla JavaScript? Why PostgreSQL instead of MongoDB? Why token-based authentication instead of session-based? Why this folder structure? How did you handle error states? What would you do differently? The ability to articulate these decisions and their trade-offs is what separates a developer who uses tools from an engineer who understands them. As you present and watch your peers present, you will see different approaches to the same challenges and learn from their solutions. The web platform continues to evolve rapidly — new APIs, new frameworks, new deployment paradigms — but the fundamentals you have learned (HTTP, DOM, component architecture, API design, database modeling, security, testing, accessibility) are durable foundations that will serve you regardless of which specific technologies you use next.
key_concepts: Full-stack integration: frontend components consume backend APIs, APIs interact with databases, authentication spans both layers, deployment connects everything to production infrastructure
Technology decisions: every choice has trade-offs; articulate what you gained and what you sacrificed with each decision; there are no universally correct answers, only well-reasoned ones
Project architecture review: does the project have clear separation of concerns? Is the code organized for maintainability? Are there automated tests? Is the deployment pipeline configured?
Live demonstration: showing working software is more convincing than slides; demonstrate the happy path, an error case, and a responsive design breakpoint
Code quality evidence: show the CI pipeline, test coverage report, Lighthouse scores, and accessibility audit results; quality is demonstrated through metrics, not claims

Peer evaluation: evaluate projects on functionality (does it work?), code quality (is it maintainable?), design (is it responsive and accessible?), and documentation (could someone else understand and extend it?)
Reflection and growth: identify what was hardest (and therefore where the most learning happened), what you would do differently, and what you want to learn next

Continuing education: the web platform evolves continuously; Server Components, edge computing, WebAssembly, and new CSS features are changing how we build; strong fundamentals enable rapid adaptation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents their final project (8 minutes): demonstrate working application with responsive design, walk through architecture (frontend, backend, database, deployment), show CI pipeline and test results, display Lighthouse and accessibility scores, and discuss the most challenging problem solved and what they would change. Class provides structured feedback using the peer evaluation rubric.

Presentation slides or live demo environment prepared

review.value: The best web developers understand the entire stack, even if they specialize. Knowing how the backend works makes you a better frontend developer (you understand what the API can and cannot do). Knowing how the frontend works makes you a better backend developer (you design APIs that are easy to consume).

Every technology choice is a trade-off. The ability to articulate 'we chose X because it optimizes for Y at the cost of Z, which is acceptable because...' is the mark of engineering maturity. Saying 'we used React because it is popular' is not a technical justification.

A deployed application that real users can access is qualitatively different from code running on localhost. Deployment surfaces real problems: CORS misconfigurations, environment variable mistakes, database connection limits, and CDN caching issues. Shipping is a skill.

The web platform changes constantly, but fundamentals are durable. HTTP, DOM, SQL, authentication, testing, and accessibility will be relevant for decades. Specific frameworks and libraries will come and go. Invest in fundamentals first, then adapt to new tools quickly.

questions.question: Explain what Express middleware is and describe the execution order when a request to GET /api/books/:id passes through: (1) a global logging middleware, (2) an authentication middleware, and (3) the route handler. What happens if the authentication middleware does not call next()?
questions.answer:
Express middleware functions receive (req, res, next) and can modify the request/response objects, end the request-response cycle by sending a response, or call next() to pass control to the next middleware in the chain.

Execution order for GET /api/books/:id:
1. Global logging middleware runs first (registered with app.use()): logs the request method and URL, calls next().
2. Authentication middleware runs second: extracts and verifies the JWT from the Authorization header, attaches user to req.user, calls next().
3. Route handler runs third: reads req.params.id, fetches the book from the database, sends the response.

If the authentication middleware does not call next(), the request-response cycle stalls. If the middleware also does not send a response (res.status(401).json(...)), the client waits indefinitely until the connection times out. If the middleware sends a 401 response without calling next(), the route handler never executes, which is the correct behavior for rejecting unauthenticated requests.

rubric.key: Explains middleware signature (req, res, next) and purpose
Correct execution order: global middleware, auth middleware, route handler
Explains that omitting next() stops the chain and potential timeout or 401 response

questions.question: What is the purpose of the key prop when rendering lists in React? What happens if you use the array index as the key, and why is this problematic?
questions.answer:
The key prop helps React identify which items in a list have changed, been added, or been removed during re-renders. React uses keys to match elements between renders and determine the minimum DOM updates needed.

Using the array index as key (key={index}) is problematic when items are reordered, inserted, or deleted. For example, if item at index 0 is deleted, all remaining items shift down by one index. React sees the same keys (0, 1, 2...) and assumes items 0, 1, 2 still exist but with different content, so it updates every item's DOM instead of removing one. This causes: (1) incorrect updates — input values or component state become associated with the wrong items, (2) unnecessary DOM mutations, and (3) subtle bugs where checked checkboxes or text inputs appear on the wrong item after reordering.

Use a stable, unique identifier (database ID, UUID) as the key instead.

rubric.key: Explains that key helps React track identity of items across re-renders
Explains the problem with index keys: state mismatch on reorder/delete

questions.question: Describe the complete JWT authentication flow from user login to authenticated API request. Include: what the client sends, what the server does, how the token is stored, and how subsequent requests are authenticated. Where should the JWT be stored on the client and why?
questions.answer:
1. Login: Client sends POST /api/auth/login with { email, password } in the request body.

2. Server validates: Server looks up the user by email, compares the password against the stored bcrypt hash using bcrypt.compare(). If invalid, returns 401 Unauthorized.

3. Token generation: Server creates a JWT with payload { sub: userId, role: 'user', exp: Date.now() + 15min }, signs it with the server's secret key using jwt.sign().

4. Token delivery: Server sets the JWT in an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie and returns 200 with user profile data.

5. Subsequent requests: Browser automatically sends the httpOnly cookie with every request to the same domain. No client-side JavaScript needed to attach the token.

6. Server verification: Authentication middleware extracts the JWT from the cookie, calls jwt.verify() with the secret key, attaches the decoded payload (user ID, role) to req.user, and calls next(). If verification fails, returns 401.

The JWT should be stored in an httpOnly cookie because: JavaScript cannot read httpOnly cookies (prevents XSS attacks from stealing the token), the browser sends it automatically (no client-side token management code), and SameSite=Strict prevents CSRF attacks.

rubric.key: Complete flow from login request through token generation to authenticated request
Correct storage recommendation (httpOnly cookie) with security justification
Mentions bcrypt for password comparison and jwt.verify for token validation

questions.question: Your React bookstore application has a Lighthouse performance score of 45. Describe four specific optimizations you would implement, and for each, explain what Core Web Vital it improves and the expected impact.
questions.answer:
1. Code splitting with React.lazy: Split the BookDetail, Cart, and Checkout routes into separate chunks loaded on demand. Improves LCP by reducing the initial JavaScript bundle size by 50-70%, so the browser parses and executes less JavaScript before rendering the first meaningful content.

2. Image optimization: Convert images to WebP format with responsive srcset sizes, add explicit width and height attributes, and add loading='lazy' for below-fold images. Improves LCP (hero image loads faster with modern format and correct size) and CLS (width/height prevent layout shifts as images load).

3. Add skeleton loading states: Replace blank content areas during data fetching with placeholder skeletons that match the final layout dimensions. Improves CLS by preventing layout shifts when data arrives and content appears, and improves perceived performance.

4. Database indexing and API caching: Add indexes on frequently queried columns (genre, author_id), add Redis caching for the /api/books list endpoint (60-second TTL). Improves LCP by reducing server response time from hundreds of milliseconds to single-digit milliseconds for cached responses.

rubric.key: Four specific, actionable optimizations (not vague suggestions)
Each optimization linked to a Core Web Vital (LCP, CLS, or INP)
Realistic impact estimates demonstrating understanding of performance causes

questions.question: Explain the N+1 query problem with a concrete database example. Show the inefficient queries and the optimized query using a JOIN.
questions.answer:
The N+1 problem: fetching a list of items (1 query) and then fetching related data for each item individually (N queries).

Example: displaying a list of 50 blog posts with author names.

Inefficient (51 queries):
  Query 1: SELECT * FROM posts ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50;
  Query 2: SELECT name FROM authors WHERE id = 1;
  Query 3: SELECT name FROM authors WHERE id = 2;
  ... (50 more queries, one per post)

Optimized (1 query with JOIN):
  SELECT posts.*, authors.name AS author_name
  FROM posts
  JOIN authors ON posts.author_id = authors.id
  ORDER BY posts.created_at DESC
  LIMIT 50;

The JOIN fetches all posts with their author names in a single query. With 50 posts, the N+1 approach makes 51 database round-trips; the JOIN makes 1. As the list grows, the N+1 approach degrades linearly while the JOIN remains constant.

rubric.key: Clearly explains N+1 pattern with concrete example showing multiple queries
Shows the correct JOIN query that solves the problem
Explains the performance difference (51 queries vs 1 query)
